g.data-narration: Welcome to Introduction to Data Analytics for IoT. This lecture covers how we transform massive IoT data into actionable insights.
g.text: Introduction to Data Analytics for IoT

g.data-narration: IoT data analytics examines and processes data from billions of connected devices to extract valuable insights for decision-making.
g.text: What is Data Analytics in IoT Context?
Process of examining, processing, and extracting insights from massive
volumes of data generated by IoT devices (sensors, actuators, smart
devices). Handles continuous streams, real-time processing, diverse

g.data-narration: IoT analytics enables predictive maintenance, smart cities, healthcare monitoring, and industrial optimization - turning raw data into business value.
Descriptive analytics answers 'What happened?' by summarizing historical data through reports, dashboards, and visualizations to identify patterns.
g.text: Summarizes historical data to identify patterns and trends. Uses data aggregation,
mining techniques. Provides operational visibility through reports and dashboards.

g.data-narration: Diagnostic analytics answers 'Why did it happen?' by drilling into root causes using correlation analysis and drill-down techniques.
g.text: Drills down to identify root causes of events. Uses drill-down, data discovery,
and correlation analysis. Provides context to descriptive findings.

g.data-narration: Predictive analytics answers 'What will happen?' using ML and statistical algorithms to forecast future outcomes like equipment failures.
g.text: Uses historical data, statistical modeling, and machine learning to forecast future
outcomes. Enables proactive decision-making with probability-based predictions.

g.data-narration: Prescriptive analytics answers 'What should we do?' by recommending optimal actions using optimization algorithms and simulation techniques.
g.text: Recommends specific actions to achieve desired outcomes. Uses optimization
algorithms and simulation. Provides automated or semi-automated recommendations.

g.data-narration: Volume refers to massive data amounts - smart cities generate 10-100 petabytes yearly, connected cars produce 4 terabytes daily per vehicle.
g.text: Three V's of IoT Data: Volume
Massive amounts of data from billions of devices. Storage infrastructure, bandwidth,
and computational demands create significant challenges.

g.data-narration: Velocity is the high speed of IoT data - autonomous vehicles generate 1GB/second, requiring real-time processing for critical decisions.
g.text: Three V's of IoT Data: Velocity
High-speed continuous data streams requiring real-time or near-real-time
processing. Millisecond to microsecond sampling rates.

g.data-narration: Variety means diverse data formats - structured sensor readings, semi-structured JSON logs, and unstructured video/audio from IoT devices.
g.text: Three V's of IoT Data: Variety
Multiple data formats from diverse heterogeneous devices requiring sophisticated

g.data-narration: Additional V's include Veracity (data quality), Variability (inconsistent flow rates), and Value (extracting actionable insights from noise).
The IoT analytics pipeline flows through seven stages: generation, collection, storage, processing, analysis, visualization, and action.
Each pipeline stage uses specific technologies: Kafka/MQTT for ingestion, HDFS/NoSQL for storage, and Spark/Storm for processing.
Edge analytics processes data locally for low-latency decisions while cloud analytics enables complex ML on historical data - hybrid approaches optimize both.
IoT analytics faces key challenges: data ingestion from millions of devices, storage scalability, processing latency, and security concerns.
Apache Hadoop handles batch processing, Spark provides fast in-memory analytics, Storm enables real-time streaming, and Flink offers true stream processing.
In summary: Four analytics types (descriptive to prescriptive), Three V's (volume, velocity, variety), and a seven-stage pipeline from generation to action.

g.text: Generation → Collection → Storage → Processing → Analysis → Viz → Action
